AppUser.title: AppUser
AppUser.description: Represents an authenticated user of the application (e.g., a teacher or administrator). Authentication details are managed by an external system (Firebase Authentication), so no sensitive credentials are stored here.

id.description: Unique identifier for the AppUser entity. This typically corresponds to the Firebase Authentication User ID (UID).

createdAt.description: Timestamp when this AppUser record was first created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when this AppUser record was last updated.

Student.title: Student
Student.description: Represents a student whose attendance is managed by the application.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Student entity.

name.description: The full name of the student.

phoneNumber.description: The student's contact phone number, used for SMS notifications.

fingerprintID.description: A unique identifier associated with the student's fingerprint, used for attendance scanning.

appUserId.description: Reference to the AppUser who manages this student. (Relationship: AppUser 1:N Student)

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the student record was first created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the student record was last updated.

AttendanceRecord.title: AttendanceRecord
AttendanceRecord.description: Represents a single attendance entry for a student on a specific date.

id.description: Unique identifier for the AttendanceRecord entity.

studentId.description: Reference to the Student whose attendance is being recorded. (Relationship: Student 1:N AttendanceRecord)

attendanceDate.description: The specific date for which the attendance is recorded.

status.description: The attendance status for the student on the given date (e.g., 'present', 'absent').

recordedAt.description: Timestamp when this attendance record was created or last updated.

definition.description: Top-level collection for application users (teachers/administrators). The document ID (`appUserId`) must match the Firebase Authentication UID. Includes timestamps for creation and updates.

params.name: appUserId
params.description: The unique identifier of the AppUser, corresponding to the Firebase Authentication UID.

definition.description: Subcollection containing student records managed by a specific AppUser. Each student document includes the denormalized 'appUserId' for authorization independence, ensuring secure owner-based access.

params.name: appUserId
params.description: The unique identifier of the managing AppUser.

params.name: studentId
params.description: The unique identifier for the Student record.

definition.description: Subcollection storing individual attendance records for a specific student. Each document must include a denormalized 'appUserId' (from the managing AppUser) and 'studentId' (from the parent Student) to ensure authorization independence and allow for robust, efficient security rules without 'get()' calls.

params.name: appUserId
params.description: The unique identifier of the managing AppUser, denormalized for authorization independence.

params.name: studentId
params.description: The unique identifier of the student for whom attendance is recorded.

params.name: attendanceRecordId
params.description: The unique identifier for the specific attendance record.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure prioritizes robust security rules, scalability, and debuggability by strictly adhering to the core design principles. The primary strategy employed is **Authorization Independence via Denormalization** and **Structural Segregation**.

**1. Authorization Independence:**
*   **AppUser:** Each `AppUser` (teacher/administrator) will have their own top-level document at `/appUsers/{appUserId}`. The `appUserId` parameter in the path will directly correspond to the Firebase Authentication UID, which also serves as the document's ID and the `id` field within the document. This makes authorization for `AppUser` documents straightforward: `request.auth.uid == appUserId`.
*   **Student:** `Student` documents are nested under the `AppUser` who manages them: `/appUsers/{appUserId}/students/{studentId}`. Crucially, each `Student` document will contain a denormalized `appUserId` field (already present in the schema). This ensures that ownership is explicit within the document itself. This allows security rules to evaluate `resource.data.appUserId == request.auth.uid` directly on the `Student` document, without needing to perform a `get()` operation on the parent `AppUser` document. This is vital for atomic operations and simplifies rule logic.
*   **AttendanceRecord:** `AttendanceRecord` documents are nested even further: `/appUsers/{appUserId}/students/{studentId}/attendanceRecords/{attendanceRecordId}`. To achieve complete authorization independence for `AttendanceRecord` documents, each document MUST include a denormalized `appUserId` field (referencing the managing `AppUser`) in addition to the `studentId` field (already present in the schema). This allows security rules for `AttendanceRecord` to verify `resource.data.appUserId == request.auth.uid` without needing to fetch the parent `Student` or `AppUser` document. This eliminates `get()` calls in rules, making them atomic, more performant, and easier to debug.

**2. Query-Aware Security Rules (QAPs) and Structural Segregation:**
*   The hierarchical structure (`/appUsers/{appUserId}/students/{studentId}/attendanceRecords/{attendanceRecordId}`) inherently supports QAPs by enforcing user-specific data isolation. A teacher (identified by `request.auth.uid`) can only list students or attendance records that reside under their own `/appUsers/{request.auth.uid}` path.
*   The denormalized `appUserId` in both `Student` and `AttendanceRecord` documents is critical for secure `list` operations. Security rules can enforce that any `list` query on `/appUsers/{appUserId}/students` or `/appUsers/{appUserId}/students/{studentId}/attendanceRecords` must include `where('appUserId', '==', request.auth.uid)`. This prevents unauthorized access or accidental exposure of data by ensuring users only retrieve data belonging to them, without the rules acting as filters (which is insecure).
*   **Deviation from User Request (Attendance Map):** The user requested an `attendance` map within the `Student` document. This approach is strongly discouraged and has been replaced by the `AttendanceRecord` subcollection. 
    *   **Reasoning:** An `attendance` map with dynamic date keys within a `Student` document would violate **Structural Segregation** (mixing student metadata with a potentially large and frequently updated list of attendance data), hinder **Authorization Independence** (difficult to apply granular rules to individual entries without `get()` calls or complex rule logic), and severely impact **Scalability and Querying** (document size limits, inefficient queries for specific dates or statuses, and problematic `list` operations). The dedicated `AttendanceRecord` subcollection, with each record as its own document, is a superior design for all these reasons, aligning with the
